title: Email Etiquette
definition: Email etiquette refers to the tone, structure, and manners expected in professional written communication.
rules: Use a clear, specific subject line: 'Budget Approval Needed by Friday' instead of just 'Question'.
Match your tone to the relationship: more formal for clients/senior leaders, relaxed but professional for close teammates.
Avoid ALL CAPS - it comes across as shouting.
Reply within a reasonable time, or send a short acknowledgment if you need more time.
Use 'CC' for people who need visibility, and 'BCC' rarely and only when appropriate.
Proofread before sending — check names, dates, and attachments.
examples:
Subject: Budget Approval Needed by Friday

Hi Raj,

Could you please review and approve the attached budget by Friday? Let me know if you have any questions.

Thanks,
Meera
Hi team, thanks for your patience — I'll share the final version by tomorrow morning.

commonMistakes.right: Urgent: Please reply by end of day.
commonMistakes.why: All-caps and multiple exclamation marks feel aggressive; state urgency calmly and clearly.
Very casual greetings can feel unprofessional in business emails, especially with clients or senior staff.

quiz.question: Which subject line is most effective?
quiz.explanation: Specific subject lines set clear expectations.

quiz.question: Why should you avoid ALL CAPS in emails?
options: It's harder to read on mobile
quiz.explanation: All caps is widely interpreted as aggressive or shouting in written communication.
